perturbation.original_text: The JVC will make and keep all books of accounts, records and reports in accordance with (i) Generally Accepted Accounting Principles and (ii) as required under the Companies Act, 1956 and other applicable laws.
perturbation.changed_text: The JVC will keep books of accounts, records, and reports that it believes are in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles and applicable sections of the Companies Act, 1956. The Company may deviate from either when justifiable reasons exist.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the JVC will keep records that 'it believes are in accordance' with accounting principles and 'applicable sections' of the Companies Act, and adding the clause 'The Company may deviate from either when justifiable reasons exist' introduces subjectivity and allows for selective compliance, making it difficult to enforce the obligation to maintain accurate and complete records.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 128 of the Companies Act, 2013 mandates that companies maintain proper books of accounts that give a true and fair view of the state of affairs of the company. The modified text allows the JVC to subjectively determine compliance and deviate from established principles, undermining the mandatory nature of maintaining accurate and complete financial records as required by the Act.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the law by introducing subjectivity and discretion where the original text mandated adherence to specific accounting principles and laws. The 'Perturbation Explanation' clearly indicates that the modified text allows for selective compliance and deviation from established principles. The 'Law Explanation' also confirms that the modified text undermines the mandatory nature of maintaining accurate and complete financial records as required by Section 128 of the Companies Act, 2013. The original text used definitive language ('will make and keep...in accordance with'), whereas the changed text introduces subjectivity ('believes are in accordance') and the possibility of deviation ('may deviate when justifiable reasons exist'). Because the scraped snippets are 'None', this determination is based on the provided 'Perturbation Explanation' and 'Law Explanation', and the original/modified text. The ambiguity created makes the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Each party hereto agrees not to transfer, sell, pledge, mortgage or make other disposition (whether by way of fixed or floating charge) or encumbrance on its shares in the JVC except as otherwise provided in this Agreement and/or except by consent of the Parties to this Agreement and in the manner laid out in the M&A.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereto should generally refrain from transferring, selling, pledging, mortgaging, or making other dispositions or encumbrances on its shares in the JVC, except as otherwise provided in this Agreement and/or with the Parties' consent. The specific procedure for any such action will be determined at the discretion of the JVC's board.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees not to' to 'should generally refrain from' weakens the prohibition on transferring shares. Adding that 'the specific procedure... will be determined at the discretion of the JVC's board' introduces ambiguity, as it does not clearly define the criteria for such discretion and potentially overrides the manner explicitly stated in M&A.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 56 of the Companies Act, 2013, deals with the transfer and transmission of securities, requiring adherence to specific procedures to protect shareholder rights. The altered text introduces ambiguities by making the prohibition of the transfer of shares less strict, and it gives discretionary authority to the board which might contradict the prescribed procedures as mandated by law and outlined in the Memorandum and Articles of Association (M&A).

CLOSE Submit The University Grants Commission Act, 1956 June 25, 2022 Table of Contents Introduction to the University Grants Commission Act Section 1. Short title and commencement Section 2. Definitions Section 3. Application of Act to institutions for higher studies other than Universities Section 4. Establishment of the Commission Section 5. Compensation of the Commission Section 6. Terms and conditions of service of members Section 7. Meetings of the Commission Section 8. Vacancies amongst members or defect in constitution not to invalidate acts or proceedings of the Commission Section 9. Temporary association of persons with the Commission for particular purposes Section 10. Staff of the Commission Section 11. Authentication of orders and other instruments of the Commission Section 12. Functions of the Commission Section 13. Inspection Section 14. Consequences of failure of Universities to comply with recommendations of the Commission Section 15. Payment of the Commission Section 16. Fund of the Commission Section 17. Budget Section 18. Annual report Section 19. Account and audit Section 20. Directions by the Central Government Section 21. Returns and information Section 22. Right to confer degrees Section 23. Prohibition of the use of word University in certain cases Section 24. Penalties Section 25. Power to make rules Section 26. Power to make regulations Introduction to the University Grants Commission Act Section 1. Short title and commencement Act No.3 of 1956 [3rd March, 1956] An Act to make provision for the co-ordination and determination of standards in Universities and for that purpose, to establish a University Grants Commission Be it enacted by Parliament in the Seventh Year of the Republic of India as follows:& mdash (1) This Act may be called the University Grants Commission Act, 1956. (2) It shall come into force on such date 1 as the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint. & mdash & mdash & mdash & mdash & mdash & mdash & ndash 1. 5th November, 1956, vide S.R.O.No.2608, dated 1st November, 1956, Gazette of India, Part II, Sec.3, p.1882 Section 2. Definitions In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,& mdash (a) “ Commission” means the University Grants Commission established under section 4 (b) “ Executive authority” , in relation to a University, means the chief executive authority of the University (by whatever name called) in which the general administration of the University is vested (c) “ Fund” means the Fund of the University Grants Commission constituted under section 16 (d) “ Member” means a member of the University Grants Commission and includes the Chairman (e) “ Prescribed” means prescribed by rules made under this Act (f) “ University” means a University established or incorporated by or under a Central Act, a Provincial Act or a State Act, and includes any such institution as may, in connection with the University concerned, be recognised by the Commission in accordance with the regulations made in this behalf under this Act. Section 3. Application of Act to institutions for higher studies other than Universities The Central Government may, on the advice of the Commission declare, by notification in the Official Gazette, that any institution for higher education, other than a University, shall be deemed to be a University for the purposes of this Act
perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a strict prohibition ('agrees not to'), while the changed text weakens this to a general suggestion ('should generally refrain from'). The added discretion given to the JVC's board ('will be determined at the discretion of the JVC's board') further undermines the original agreement. The law explanation suggests that Section 56 of the Companies Act, 2013 requires adherence to specific procedures to protect shareholder rights; the modified text's ambiguity and board discretion may contradict these prescribed procedures, creating a legal contradiction. The snippets provided do not relate to this act, but the LLM explanation suggests a contradiction, and the definition of Ambiguities Legal Contradiction specifies that this kind of ambiguity creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that they will render full co-operation and execute all necessary documents, including consents and no-objection certificates to the Government of India, to ensure that all necessary government approvals can be procured to give effect to the provisions of this Article.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties will attempt to cooperate and execute documents, including consents and no-objection certificates to the Government of India, as reasonably possible, considering their individual business priorities, to facilitate government approvals as deemed relevant.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will render full cooperation' to 'will attempt to cooperate as reasonably possible' weakens the commitment to cooperation. Adding 'considering their individual business priorities' introduces a subjective element that could justify non-cooperation. Replacing 'ensure that all necessary government approvals can be procured' with 'facilitate government approvals as deemed relevant' makes the commitment to obtaining approvals discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 447 of the Companies Act, 2013, outlines the punishment for fraud, which can include acts intended to deceive or obstruct lawful procedures for obtaining required approvals. By weakening the cooperation commitment and introducing subjectivity regarding the relevance of government approvals, the changed text potentially enables actions that could be construed as attempts to avoid or circumvent regulatory requirements. This might obstruct the lawful regulatory processes, and create situations where fraud may occur, which the law is designed to prevent.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE - 15 EVENTS OF DEFAULT AND CONSEQUENT TRANSFER OF SHARES 15.2 b)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original commitment to full cooperation by introducing subjective considerations ('as reasonably possible, considering their individual business priorities') and discretionary elements ('facilitate government approvals as deemed relevant'). This directly contradicts the original text's emphasis on ensuring government approvals. Although no specific law snippets were provided, the Law Explanation argues this weakening could potentially enable actions construed as attempts to circumvent regulatory requirements, obstructing lawful regulatory processes. The Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition indicates that introducing an obligation ambiguously makes it difficult to enforce under law and may result in non-compliance. This aligns with the changes that make cooperation less mandatory and more subject to the parties' interpretations.
